title: Build a Complete Vision Pipeline — Capstone

questions.question: Why do production vision pipelines use Pydantic models (or equivalent) at every stage boundary?
options: Pydantic is faster than dict access
It helps with GPU allocation
Pydantic is required by FastAPI

Typed schemas catch interface mismatches at the boundary; a detector returning (cx, cy, w, h) when the downstream expects (x1, y1, x2, y2) fails loudly with a validation error instead of silently producing empty crops
questions.explanation: Silent interface bugs are the defining pain of ML pipelines. Every boundary has a coordinate system, a channel order, a value range, and a shape. Validating at the boundary with Pydantic turns every mismatch into an immediate error. The cost is a few microseconds; the savings are hours of debugging.

questions.question: The 7-stage pipeline (decode → preprocess → detect → crop → classify → aggregate → validate) labels which two stages as 'expensive'?
options: Preprocessing and postprocessing (NMS, RLE) — both are CPU-bound
Aggregation and schema validation — they touch the most data
The detector (YOLO / Mask R-CNN) and the classifier (ConvNeXt-Tiny) — both are model inference stages; the five non-model stages are cheaper but are where interface bugs live
Preprocessing and the detector — preprocessing dominates on CPU
questions.explanation: The doc explicitly marks the detector and classifier stages as expensive (filled nodes in the Mermaid diagram) because they run neural network forward passes. The remaining five stages — decode, crop, aggregate, validate, respond — are where the bugs live, but they are not the latency bottleneck on GPU.

questions.question: The VisionPipeline.preprocess() method accepts a PIL Image or numpy HWC uint8 array. What does it return?
options: A CHW float32 tensor normalised to [0, 1] and moved to the configured device — the standard format expected by torchvision detection models
A dict with 'image' and 'shape' keys compatible with Pydantic
A list of resized crops ready for the classifier
A PIL Image rescaled to the model's minimum input size
questions.explanation: preprocess() converts to RGB via PIL, casts to float with torch.from_numpy, reorders channels from HWC to CHW with .permute(2,0,1), divides by 255.0, and moves the tensor to self.device. This matches what torchvision detection models expect as input.

questions.question: The 'Where latency goes' section states three truths. Which correctly describes GPU postprocessing (NMS, RLE) vs CPU postprocessing?
options: Postprocessing always dominates because it follows every forward pass
NMS and RLE encoding are cheap on GPU but expensive on CPU; always profile with the actual deployment target before optimising
Preprocessing dominates GPU time more than the detector forward pass
Postprocessing cost is negligible everywhere and can be ignored in benchmarks
questions.explanation: The doc states postprocessing is 'cheap on GPU, expensive on CPU'. NMS is parallelisable and vectorisable on GPU but sequential on CPU. The lesson's benchmark function measures preprocess, detect, and classify stages separately so you can see the real distribution on your hardware before deciding what to optimise.

questions.question: The pipeline classifier crashes when a detection has box size 3×4 pixels. What is the correct fix in VisionPipeline?
options: Add a global try/except and return empty classifications

Set a min_crop threshold (e.g. 32 pixels) and skip classification for detections smaller than that; include the detection in the response without a classification entry so the output is complete and honest
Retrain the classifier on smaller images
Increase batch size to dilute the bad crop
questions.explanation: The VisionPipeline constructor accepts min_crop (default 32). In run(), any crop with width or height below min_crop is skipped: it is excluded from crops[] and valid_indices[], so classify() never sees it. The detection is still added to detections[]; the caller gets a full result with a missing classification entry rather than a crash.

questions.question: Your FastAPI endpoint returns a 500 error when a user uploads a corrupted JPEG. How should you fix it?
options: Ignore it; the user will retry with the same file
Move image decoding inside the classifier to isolate failures
Always return 200 with an empty detections list

Catch image-decode failures early in preprocessing and return HTTP 400 with a specific error code like 'image_decode_failed' — 500 implies a server fault and triggers client retry with the same bad input, amplifying load
questions.explanation: A 500 from bad user input is an API contract bug. 4xx signals 'client problem, don't retry with the same data'; 5xx signals 'server problem, retry.' The FastAPI service in the lesson catches Image.open failures and raises HTTPException(status_code=400). Named failure codes (image_decode_failed, unsupported_content_type) let clients react correctly and log precisely.
